content.full_text:
City of Alameda California

[illegible]
MAR I 8 09 AM '91

March 5, 1991

General Counsel
Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807


Re:  Request for Formal Written Advice for City Planning
     Board Members Mrs. Pritchard and Mr. Appezzato

Dear Madam or Sir:

     Mrs. Linda Pritchard is the president and Mr. Ralph Appezzato
is a member of the City of Alameda's Planning Board. On behalf of
Mrs. Pritchard and Mr. Appezzato, I am hereby requesting a formal
advice letter from the FPPC on their ability to participate in
decisions of the Planning Board related to adoption of the City's
proposed redevelopment plan and implementation of specific
redevelopment programs and projects.

     The City of Alameda Planning Board is made up seven members.
The City Charter requires a majority vote of the seven members in
order for the Board to take action. Duties of the Planning Board
include taking action related to the City's proposed redevelopment
plan for the Business and Waterfront Improvement Project.

     The Business and Waterfront Redevelopment Area includes the
major older commercial and industrial areas of the City. Overall,
the proposed area comprises about 10% of the City's total area and
58% of the City's commercial and industrial areas. In order to
accomplish comprehensive revitalization of the older commercial and
industrial areas of the City, the proposed redevelopment area
includes two major areas. A copy of a map of the proposed area is
attached for your reference. More detailed maps of the specific
areas involved are also attached. Major projects proposed for the
entire area include commercial building rehabilitation and facade
improvements, transportation improvements, storm drainage projects,
curb and gutter improvements, public parking improvements,
expansion of civic facilities, recreational open space and low and
moderate income housing improvements.

_______________  Office of the City Attorney, Room 314

City Hall
Santa Clara Avenue at Oak Street · 94501
415.748 4544

FPPC Legal Division
March 5, 1991
Page 2


The particular facts relating to Mrs. Pritchard and Mr.
Appezzato are separately set forth below.

Mrs. Pritchard

Mrs. Pritchard is the current president of the Planning Board.
Her term as president expires in July of this year, but she will
continue to be a member of the Board.  Mrs. Pritchard is a real
estate broker by profession.  She owns 25% of the real estate
company and may receive a percentage of the sales commissions (minus
expenses) made by other agents in the company.  The company is
located on property it owns.  The property is valued between
$10,000.00 and $100,000.00.  The property is located at 2515
Clement Street which is in the redevelopment area and zoned M-2,
General Industrial (Manufacturing).  This zone allows most uses
except residential.  (A copy of the City's basic rules for the M-2
zone is attached.)

Mrs. Pritchard specializes in residential properties.  In the
past and presumably in the future some of the properties she
receives commissions from may be located in the redevelopment area.

Mrs. Pritchard also owned residential rental property located
on 9th Street near the redevelopment area.  As of October 10, 1989
and as part of a divorce settlement she no longer owns nor has any
current interest in this property.

Mr. Appezzato

Mr. Appezzato sold his residence located on Yorkshire Place
to Anthony and Christina Poggio in March, 1990.  Yorkshire Place
is not in or near the proposed redevelopment area.  The Poggios
executed a promissory note in the amount of $153,500.00 to the
Appezzatos for a portion of the purchase price.  The promissory
note is secured by a deed of trust on Dr. Poggio's former residence
at 1612 Wood Street.  (A copy of the note and deed of trust are
attached.)

The Wood Street home is located outside of the proposed area
but within 300 feet of a portion of the redevelopment area which
connects the major segments of the redevelopment area.  Wood
Street is a residential area zoned R-2.  The R-2 density allows up
to two units per lot.  (A copy of the City's basic rules for R-2
zone is attached.)  Wood Street is generally a single family
residential neighborhood.  Wood Street connects to Lincoln Avenue
which is in the redevelopment area.  In this area, Lincoln Street

FPPC Legal Division
March 5, 1991
Page 3


is zoned R-2 with a small block of C-1.  The C-1, Neighborhood
Business, zone allows limited commercial uses which serve the
nearby residential zones.  At present, this area of Lincoln is
mainly developed with single family residential uses.  There are
only two commercial uses in the area: a shoe store and a liquor
store.  Both appear to be commercially viable.  There are no
projects planned for this commercial area.  This area of Lincoln
Avenue area is clean and reasonably well maintained.  The only
redevelopment project proposed for this area would include
replacement of its minor areas of curb and gutter improvements
several blocks east along Lincoln.

     Under the terms of the promissory note, Dr. Poggio has been
making payments of interest.  The payments Mr. Appezzato has
received from Dr. Poggio in the last 12 months exceed $250.00.

     Last month, the Appezzatos and the Poggios renegotiated the
terms of the note to extend the due date, increase the interest
rate and provide additional security for the note.  The additional
security is a deed of trust on the Yorkshire Place residence.  The
amended note provides that the principal shall be due in full upon
the earlier of October 1, 1994 or the sale of the Wood Street
property.  In addition, the parties agreed that in the event of
foreclosure, Mr. Appezzato would foreclose on the Wood Street
property before he foreclosed on the Yorkshire Place property.  The
reason for amending the original agreement was because Dr. Poggio
was having difficulty selling the Wood Street property due to the
sluggish real estate market.  Dr. Poggio is currently using the
Wood Street house as rental property and receives rental income
from it.

     Mr. Appezzato believes that the deed of trust on the Yorkshire
Place property provide more than enough security for the note; the
appraised value of the Yorkshire Place home is $347,000.00.  The
lender's first deed of trust on that property is $180,000.00,
leaving a balance of $167,000.00 to secure the note to Mr.
Appezzato.  This is $14,000.00 more than is needed to secure the
note.

     In summary, 1612 Wood Street is located in a primarily
residential area and there are no redevelopment projects planned
for this area.  It is located within 300 feet of a secluded portion
of the programmed redevelopment area, where little redevelopment
activity will take place.  Only minor public improvements to street
rights-of-way are contemplated as projects in this area.  It

FPPC Legal Division
March 5, 1991
Page 4


appears there would be no material financial impact on any
residence in the Lincoln Street area including 1612 Wood Street.

Along with the documents described above, I have enclosed
photographs of the Wood Street area. I have also enclosed a copy
of a statement provided by Mr. Appezzato.

I would appreciate your response as soon as possible since
the Planning Board will be making decisions regarding the
redevelopment plan in the near future. If you need any additional
information, please feel free to contact me at (415) 748-4544.


Very Truly Yours,

[signature]
Heather C. Mc Laughlin
Assistant City Attorney

enclosures

cc:  Mrs. Pritchard (without enclosures)
     Mr. Appezzato (without enclosures)
     City Attorney (without enclosures)
     Economic Development Manager (without enclosures)
     Planning Director (without enclosures)
     Iris Yang (without enclosures)

City of Alameda California

via fax and mail

July 12, 1991

Bianca Breeze
Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request for Formal Written Advice for City Planning
Board Members Mrs. Pritchard and Mr. Appezzato

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This letter is a follow up to our recent conversation about
the request for formal written advice for City Planning Board
members Mrs. Pritchard and Mr. Appezzato (your file number 91-141).

Since the Planning Board is in the process of appointing
members to a committee to work on specific plans for certain areas
of the City, it is necessary to have a determination by the Fair
Political Practices Commission about the potential conflict of
interest of Mrs. Pritchard and Mr. Appezzato.

It is expected that the specific plan committee will be made
up of three members of the Planning Board and three members of the
City's Economic Development Commission. Several specific plans are
called for by the City's General Plan. Each of the plan areas
involve land in the redevelopment area. The scope of each specific
plan will vary according to the perceived needs of the areas
involved. Generally speaking, the plans will address urban design,
economic and transportation issues and land uses. The attached
memo from the Planning Director to the City Manager dated May 29,
1991 describes in detail the differences between the plan areas.
The specific plan committee was constituted to formulate a
recommendation to the Planning Board regarding the scope of work
for each specific plan and the priority or scheduling of the
preparations. After these tasks are completed, the committee will
be disbanded. It is proposed that the specific plans be prepared
by planning consulting firms. The plans will be presented to the
Planning Board, and the Board will make a recommendation to the
City Council concerning adoption of the plans.

Office of the City Attorney, Room 314

City Hall
Santa Clara Avenue at Oak Street · 94501
415.748 4544

The image appears to be mostly blank or very faintly marked, with only minimal visible content in the upper right corner that is too unclear to transcribe with certainty. The rest of the page is white/empty.

Bianca Breeze
July 12, 1991
Page 2


     As noted in my previous letter, Mrs. Pritchard's interest
involves property located on Clement Street. This area would most
likely be involved in the Park Street specific plan. Mr.
Appezzato's interest involves property located on Wood Street which
is not included in any specific plan area but is located within 300
feet of the redevelopment area.

     I would appreciate your response as soon as possible since
the specific plan committee's first meeting is set for July 19,
1991. If you need any additional information, please feel free to
contact me at (415) 748-4544.

                                        Very Truly Yours,

                                        Heather C. McLaughlin
                                        Assistant City Attorney

enclosure

cc:  Mrs. Pritchard
     Mr. Appezzato
     City Attorney
     Planning Director
     Economic Development Manager

City of Alameda
Inter-department Memorandum


To:     William C. Norton

From:   De Wayne Guy, Planning Director

Date:   May 29, 1991

Subject: Scheduling of specific plans called for by the General
         Plan

     The General Plan calls for the preparation of specific plans
for:

     *   the Webster St. Business District (IP 2.5.h)* and

     *   the Civic Center (IP 3.4.d), and also calls for various
         implementing actions that suggest a specific plan for

     *   the Park St. Business District (IP 2.5.i - 2.5.o), as
         well as one or more specific plans for the Specified
         Mixed Use Areas (MU1, MU2, MU4 and MU5), in

     *   the Northern Waterfront area (IP 2.6.g - 2.6.j).

     This memo constitutes a very preliminary work program  or
schedule for preparing the specific plans, as well as other
related planning tasks. Several factors were considered in
developing this schedule:

A.   the occurrence of other key events and processes,

B.   the level of need or urgency in each plan area,

C.   the scope of proposed planning in each plan area,

D.   certain immediate impediments to planning in each plan
     area, and

E.   the availability of staff resources.

F.   the availability of fiscal resources.

     Each of these factors is discussed below, then the proposed
schedule is put forth.

I.  Considerations that affect the scheduling of the specific
    plans

     A.  Three events and three processes will affect establishing
         a schedule for these specific plans.

* refers to General Plan Implementing Policies.


Printed on recycled paper

Specific Plane-WCN

1. The Civic Center Plan should not be commenced until
a library project has either been approved and
committed to or abandoned. This remains unresolved
until our grant application has been finally acted
upon. There may also be an unknown period following
a favorable announcement, while the extent and
method of City participation is debated.

2. Several major employers in the Northern Waterfront
area have publicly opposed efforts to plan for new
uses in this area. Planning here should not be
commenced until this opposition has been
substantially reduced.

3. All of the specific plans will require funding from
the Business and Waterfront Improvement Project
(BWIP), and thus cannot commence until this plan
goes into effect, which is anticipated in mid-July,
1991.

4. Comprehensive rezoning, involving both text and map
changes, will follow the General Plan adoption.
This will be a significant process, involving the
whole City, and taking perhaps as much as two years
to complete. Map changes will be involved in both
the Park St. and Webster St. areas, and should be
coordinated with those specific planning efforts.

5. Any specific planning along the Northern Waterfront
should be closely coordinated with the Estuary
rezoning and Shoreline Access Plan. These planning
efforts should begin by April or May of this year,
and will probably take over a year to complete.

6. Consideration of an amendment of the General Plan,
to address the introduction of administrative and
professional (AP) offices in residential areas, will
commence in June. This process would take at least
six months to complete. It would definitely affect
the comprehensive rezoning, and may affect the
specific plans for both Park St. and Webster St.

B. The urgency of having specific plan direction in
particular areas is also an important factor in the four
planning areas is as follows:

1. Webster Street appears to have the greatest need for
immediate planning. The business district is
floundering, and there are a number of approved and

2

Specific Plans-WCN

potential projects in this area that need
integration. The Island Auto Movie Specified Mixed
Use (MUI) area should be considered with the Webster
St. plan because of their mutual impacts.

2. Civic Center also has a great need for a coordinated
plan to tie the several public facilities together,
and to make a clearer physical statement of
municipal focus.

3. The Northern Waterfront is in flux, and has perhaps
the greatest potential in the city for accommodating
new uses. Many people feel that some sort of new
direction, different from that portrayed in the
General Plan, shoul.d be established for it. There
have been recent indications of new development in
the Encinal Terminals area. Such development will
need both General Plan and zoning changes that
should be made only after the whole area has been
studied. Transportation issues are particularly
important.

4. Park Street as a commercial area also needs
attention, but not as much as Webster St. does.
Park St. could perhaps be considered together with
the Civic Center area because of their proximity.

C. The scope of proposed planning in each of the plan areas
has an affect on the length of time each planning process
will take.

1. The Webster St. specific plan would involve social
and economic issues, as well as transportation, land
use and urban design. The General Plan proposes to
analyse the commercial area, requiring a lot by lot
analysis, and eventual rezoning. I recommend
including the MUI (Island Auto Movie) site with this
plan, and the Planning Board recommendation also
calls for the landscaping and design plan for the
Webster/Posey Tubes to be included.

This specific plan will be a major undertaking.
The time needed to complete it will exceed a year,
but would need to be addressed more definitively as
part of the RFP/scoping process.

2. The Civic Center specific plan would be primarily
a classical urban design effort, with minimal

3

Specific Plans-WCN


social, economic and land use issues.  There is a
request from the Historical Advisory Board (HAB) to
expand the plan area to include Historic Alameda
High School, and some other historic buildings in
the plan area.  There may be some controversy about
this, and possibly other historic issues that have
not yet surfaced.  For that reason this plan could
take as much as a year to complete.

3.  Specific planning in the Northern Waterfront area
would involve social and economic issues, as well
as transportation, land use and design.  It would
be controversial and of considerable interest
throughout the community.  The Specified Mixed Use
areas (MU4 and 5) as well as possibly MU2 may be
considered together, as one large area, or
separately.  I recommend that MU1 be included with
the Webster St. Plan.  The Encinal Terminals area
should also be studied, though it was not identified
by the General Plan as a Specified Mixed Use area.
Between one and three years would be needed to
involve all the interested parties and to develop
a strong plan.

4.  The Park St. specific plan would involve social and
economic issues, as well as transportation, land use
and design.  It too may have broad community
interest, but the issues are less complex than
Webster St. and the Northern Waterfront.  It would
probably take about one year to complete this plan.

D.  There are some factors that make it difficult to proceed
immediately with planning in two of the areas.

1.  In the Civic Center area there has been some
sensitivity and controversy around the proper
location for the library.  This issue should not be
raised in a Planning process until the grant
application has been acted upon.

2.  In the Northern Waterfront area many of the key
employers have indicated no desire to see a change
of direction at this time.  Also, those interested
in change do not seem to have clear or unified
objectives, although this may be changing.


4

Specific Plans-WCN


3.      There are no significant factors that would hold up
        planning efforts in either the Webster St. or Park
        St. areas.


E.     Availability of staff resources is a key consideration
       in developing a schedule for the specific plans.  Staff
       will be needed to prepare RFP's and to administer the
       resulting contracts for the specific plans.  Although
       consultants would be used to carry the main weight of
       these plans, we need a continuing, significant
       involvement of staff to insure that local concerns and
       interests are well represented in the process and final
       products.

       There is currently one person available for long-range
       planning, which includes General Plan amendments and
       comprehensive rezoning, as well as coordinating the
       specific plans.  Unless more staff could be made
       available, we should not attempt to work on more than one
       specific plan at a time.

       However, as previously discussed, there is a need to
       coordinate the comprehensive rezoning and A-P General
       Plan amendment with both the Webster St. and Park St.
       Plans.  Careful scheduling will be needed to avoid
       working on more than one specific plan at a time and thus
       overloading the one staff person.  In any case there will
       be severe long-range planning staffing problems in the
       next two years.

F.     Availability of fiscal resources for preparing the
       specific plans is another consideration.  All of these
       plans will require outside consultants, and will be
       substantial elements of the Planning Department budget.
       While the availability of funds is not known at this
       time, these efforts may need to be spread out over time
       to be able to afford them.


II.    Proposed schedule for starting and preparing plans

       Based on the above factors, I recommend the following schedule
       for preparing the specific plans.

A.     Webster St. is the strongest candidate for an early
       launch.  There are no special timing constraints on this
       area, and its urgency, as a demonstration of City

                                    5

Specific Plans-WCN


interest in its potential, is the greatest of the four
areas. It could not commence before mid-summer because
of funding limitations, and because planning staff is
currently fully occupied starting processes for
comprehensive rezoning, A/P in residential areas and
Estuary Rezoning.

The plan for this area should be coordinated with the
possible General Plan amendments for commercial intrusion
into residential areas. It should also be done at the
same time planning is done for Mixed Use area MU1 (Island
Auto Movie site). The time needed to complete it would
probably exceed one year, but this is speculative at this
point, and would need to be addressed more definitely
as part of the RFP/scoping process.

B. The Civic Center Plan is the next strongest candidate.
If the Library is given the go-ahead, it would become a
"given" in an area that needs a plan to deal with such
issues as the best strategy for providing parking, as
well as rehabilitating and reusing the Carnegie Building.

If the new library does not go, it would be a good time
to look at how the area should be tied together with the
existing issues and resources, and how the needs of the
library could then be met in the area. However, the
sense of urgency may be less in this case.

This plan could not be started before mid-summer (1991)
at the earliest. In this case, the RFP should not be
prepared or circulated until the fate of the Library in
well known. This plan could be completed in less than
a year, perhaps as little as six months.

C. The issues delaying planning in the Northern Waterfront
may be cleared up within the next year. This area could
become a very strong candidate for planning as soon as
that happens. The Specified mixed use areas may be
combined into one or more separate planning efforts,
which would have a significant effect on timing. I have
recommended that MU1 be combined with the Webster St.
plan. The Terminal Terminals area should also be included
in this plan, though it is not specifically called for
in the General Plan.

Between one and three years would be needed to involve
all the interested parties and to develop a strong plan.

6

Specific Plans-WCN


D.    It could be argued that Park St. should be considered
     along with the Civic Center area because of their
     proximity.  However, Park St. itself has the lowest
     urgency.  It would take perhaps a year to complete this
     plan.

     If these plans were undertaken sequentially, they would take
     approximately four to six years.  The attached preliminary
     time-line reflects the most optimistic scenario. If Park St.
     were combined with Civic Center that time could perhaps be
     reduced by a month to one year. If the Northern Waterfront
     were done in one large plan, perhaps another year could be
     saved.  Even quicker results could be achieved by working on
     two or more plans at once, but as discussed before, that is
     not recommended with current staffing.

     In summary, I recommend beginning to prepare the RFP for the
     Webster St./MU1 plan as soon as the AP study and comprehensive
     rezoning are underway, probably by July of this year.  This
     plan could be begun in the fall (1991), and would take over
     a year.  The RFP for the Civic Center plan could be prepared
     and circulated prior to completing the Webster St./MU1 plan.
     We could then be ready to start the Civic Center plan in the
     spring of 1993.  This plan will take about a year, and could
     be completed by the following spring.  By that time, two years
     from now, the Northern Waterfront situation should be clearer.
     Depending on the strategy for approaching this area, planning
     would possibly be complete around spring of 1995.  The Park
     St. plan could run concurrently with the Northern Waterfront
     plan, and could also be complete by the spring of 1995.



                                    7

[The image appears to be mostly blank/white with very faint text that is not legible enough to transcribe accurately. There are some barely visible marks or text near the top and middle of the page, but they are too faint or unclear to read with confidence.]
